title: Why Chinese women made Sherlock Holmes a gay icon

transcript.text: Chinese tourists are visiting London for
reasons the locals wouldn't necessarily
understand. Some, for example, are drawn
by their love of the relationship
between Sherlock Holmes and his
sidekick, Dr. Watson. And by
relationship, I mean a romantic one. In
China, it's not unusual for super fans
to [music] invent elaborate love stories
between their favorite characters in
popular shows or to give them endearing
nicknames.
>> Curly and Peanut. So, so, so who, so
what do those words mean? And, and, and,
and, and, and, and, and, and, and, and,
and, and, and, and, and who is Curly Fu
and who is Peanut?
>> So, these are the nicknames that fans
have given them. Curly refers to
Benedict's uh, really curly hair, which
the character Sherlock also shares. Fu
is short for homes.
So, it's fu. And then peanut is really
just a word play on Watson which is
which sounds like peanut. And the the
amazing thing is these both these actors
have gone on to do a great variety of
works in theater and in TV and films but
they are to this day still known as
Curly Fu and Peanut.
>> Okay. So it sounds like something a bit
different is going on here. So tourists
have always been drawn to Britain
because of its sort of traditional
culture and sort of classic works of
literature. So they're interested
>> and literature. Yeah.
>> Yes. Jane Austin and Shakespeare plays
and and that sort of thing. But Sherlock
is a TV series set in modern Britain.
>> Yeah.
>> So and it sounds like the visitors are
really more interested in the actors
than they are in sort of Saratha Conand
Doyle's stories perhaps.
>> Yes. the lines quite blurry you'll find
with true fandom it was a huge show in
China when it came out um and I remember
when the then prime minister David
Cameron visited China official trip of
course uh but he opened up an account on
way China's ex to sort of interact with
Chinese public and the top request for
him was to speed up the release of the
new season of Sherlock. No one was
actually interested in talking politics
with him. It was all like, "When's the
new Sherlock coming out?" And so, it
holds a very special place. I grew up
reading a lot about Romeo, Juliet,
Elizabeth, and Mr. Darcy. Those were the
love stories that I knew. But for a lot
of younger Chinese people,
Sherlock and Watson is the ultimate UK
love story.
>> Okay. So, there's a love story between
Sherlock Holmes and Watson. Yes.
>> I mean, those those two characters to me
don't even seem to like each other, let
alone love each other.
>> Well, it's funny you say that because in
the series, I think Dr. Watson's married
and, you know, Sherlock has his own love
interest, but the Chinese fans have sort
of curated this whole plotline of um
Sherlock and Watson being the the one
true love of one another. And it really
launched what we call in China the
rotten women phenomenon, which is the
fandom that depicts romantic and even
sexual relationships between men, but it
is largely written by and for straight
women.
>> So that sounds a little bit like what's
often called slash fiction. There are
many examples of that. A particularly
popular one over the decades has been
imagining relationships between um
Captain Kirk and Dr. Spock, the two main
characters on Star Trek.
>> Wow, you seem very well-versed. Are you
a runw woman by any extent? [laughter]
>> What's the appeal of these of these
ideas for these women? Do you think
>> it feels like from a female perspective
if it's two men that are in a romantic
relationship, it subverts the
traditional power dynamics between men
and women and you know it's established
that neither of them can bear children.
Not having mother-in-law drama or
thinking about bride prize, love just
becomes purely about love.
I can remember a few years ago you and I
going to Shandong in in China and
interviewing people who we found on the
street about marriage. And the
impression I got was just how
extraordinarily difficult and effortful
marriage was. I mean, nobody ever said
that love and marriage were easy, but
they should be fun. And for the people
we met in Shandong, it was really not
fun. Um the men were having a very very
hard time finding partners because there
were too many of them. Women were under
great pressure for other sorts of
reasons.
>> Yeah. There's almost this toxicity
around marriage or a male female
relationship. You know, we did that
report in 2017, but a lot of that
dynamic still haven't changed. you still
you're still dealing with a really
skewed sex ratio and it's even harder
for people to want to get married or
have children. It's not really good news
for the state because they obviously
want more babies. And so in recent
years, you've actually seen them coming
out cracking down on bride prices as
well as a lot of this gay fanfiction um
because it it doesn't fit within their
values and what they want to promote.
Yes, but people will always sort of
imagine stories. It's very it's it's
very hard to prevent people from
imagining things, I suppose.
>> Thank you, Joel, for joining me on Drum
Tower today. We'd love to have you back
soon.
>> Thank you.
[music]
